01
Chapter 01 · Getty Images / iStock

Generate now, license later: making AI reference images feel free without hiding the bill

Sole content designer on the working team · April 2024 – May 2025 · Shipped on two brands
The 60-second versionfull chapter ≈ 4 min
The problem
Let users generate with stock images they haven't licensed yet — on a platform whose whole business is licensing. Nobody gets to feel trapped.
The move
A locked vocabulary, disabled states that give the reason, and a CTA pair that leads with freedom: "Generate now, license later."
The result
Shipped on two brands. In testing: 86% comprehension, 100% downloaded. The pattern set became the foundation of Getty's next product.

The challenge

Getty Images' commercially safe AI generator needed reference images: pair a text prompt with an existing image and choose whether it influences the color or the composition of a new generation. The hardest phase let users reference stock images they hadn't licensed yet — on a platform whose entire business is licensing.

Research kept returning one theme: users didn't resent paying — they resented finding out late.

"Bait-and-switch" paywalls were the top trust killer. So the brief became: let someone generate freely with an image they haven't paid for, make the eventual license requirement unmissable, and never once feel like a trap.

My role

Sole content designer with two product designers, a researcher, and a PO. I owned the copy system end to end — feature copy, education, error/empty/disabled states — plus launch QA, research distillation, upload legal and privacy language, and stakeholder walkthrough scripts.

More than the strings: I owned the strategy for how users would understand reference images at all — progressive disclosure of the right information at the right moment, reasoned and researched, coordinated across design, engineering, and the model vendor. We A/B-tested designs and content variants with our researcher, and I presented direction up to the executive level through QA and launch.

Terminology systemsTrust & licensing UXResearch translationLaunch QA

Locking the vocabulary

The first job was naming the two influence options. I rejected "Influence priority" (names the mechanism, not the choice) and "Select what to take from your reference image" ("take" is too aggressive for a creative tool).

We landed on Focus on color ("Transfer color palette and tone") and Focus on composition ("Transfer compositional elements like pose").

When review feedback showed users didn't realize the onboarding examples were new generations, I iterated the step heading until it taught the causality itself: "Select what to focus on and generate."

Disabled states that give the reason, not the shrug

With a reference active, camera controls and aspect ratios lock. The default copy said "Unavailable" — which implies broken. I set the rule: "disabled" (intentional, temporary), never "unavailable" (missing) — and always state why.

Exhibit 1A — Disabled-state copy. Start with what shipped before I got there, then reveal the fix.
ControlBeforeAfter — states the technical truth
Camera controlsUnavailable when using a reference imageCamera controls disabled when using a reference image for best results.
Aspect ratios🚫 icon + "…to maintain the highest quality"Aspect ratios disabled when using a reference image to prevent partial image use or distortion.
Color & moodWhile using a reference image for color, this setting may not apply as expectedColor settings may be affected by reference image.

The licensing flow: clarity couldn't override truth

The unlicensed-reference flow had four beats to land, in order: a reference was used; you must license it to download; licensing supports the original creator and gets you both images; pricing is on the next screen.

The hardest decision was rejecting "Use without licensing" as a CTA — even though it tested as the clearest — because it implied users could proceed and assume the copyright risk themselves, which is legally wrong.

I also banned "unlock" (paywall vocabulary), banned "free" anywhere (generating still costs credits), and rejected "Pre-shot images" as a tab label (ambiguous).

User testing showed people assumed limited functionality without upfront licensing. So the CTA pair evolved to "Generate now, license later" / "License now", equal prominence — leading with full functionality, because research kept surfacing a trial-first mindset.

Legal confirmed the exact moment of licensing didn't matter, only that the requirement was always clear — which freed the flow to stay friction-light.

Use this image as a reference?
How it works

Use this image's colors or composition to generate a new image — and get started right away.

Licensing requirements

To download your final generated image, you'll need to license this reference image. View pricing details

Generate now, license laterLicense now
This generated image used a reference image

To download your generated image, you'll need to license the reference image. Licensing supports the original creator and allows you to download both the reference image and your generated image.

License to download

Pricing details will be shown on the next screen.

Exhibit 1B — Recreated confirmation modal and download interruption. The header is deliberately neutral and urgency-free (returning users genuinely forget); the value framing turns an obligation into an exchange.

Exhibit 1C — Where the language moved the design

Content decisions don't stop at the string. What each one changed in the UI — and who was in the room.

"Generate now, license later" / "License now" had to read as options, not a recommendation
→
The modal ships both CTAs at equal visual weight — the button hierarchy itself stopped steering.With the product designers, off A/B-tested content variants
"Disabled, never unavailable" — and always say why
→
Locked controls became a designed pattern: every restriction overlay carries its reason, no bare 🚫 icons.With design and engineering
Legal's ruling: the moment of licensing doesn't matter, only that the requirement is always clear
→
The flow stayed friction-light — the requirement rides along as persistent copy instead of gating screens.Negotiated with legal; a copy question that decided the flow architecture

Outcomes

  • Phase 1 shipped on both Getty Images and iStock in July 2024; the uploads phase went kickoff to dev-ready in six weeks; the unlicensed-reference direction locked in November 2024 and entered build in January 2025.
  • In usability testing of the reference-image experience: 86% comprehension, 72% felt in control of outcomes, 100% downloaded an image. One of eight features I landed across Getty & iStock.
  • Launch QA caught two cross-brand blockers before release.
  • The pattern set — reference mental models, license-later flows, restriction overlays — became the foundation of Getty's next-generation prompt-based editing product. Chapter 02 is its sequel.

"Clarity can't override truth. The clearest label in the test was still the wrong one — the craft is finding the phrasing that's both."What Chapter 01 taught me

02
Chapter 02 · Getty Images / iStock

A language system for AI editing that tells the truth

Content designer, prompt-based editing · 2025 – 2026 · The sequel to Chapter 01 — two years of continuity on one product family
The 60-second versionfull chapter ≈ 4 min
The problem
An AI enhancement could quietly change your picture — textures, skin, a tooth. Internal QA knew. The download screen didn't.
The move
Three verbs that carry scope, a deliberately robotic response vocabulary, and download copy rewritten from selling to describing.
The result
Vocabulary locked into engineering handoff. The honest-framing method became the team's review format. The copy pass caught a production bug.

The challenge

Getty's prompt-based editing product layered natural-language editing onto Getty and iStock: type "make it night" and the image changes. The catch lived in the model stack — the AI enhancement that produced the larger files stock customers expect could literally change the picture. Internal QA had flagged altered textures, skin, even a tooth gap.

And usage data showed users gravitate to the largest download option — exactly the one most likely to change their image.

Three problems stacked:

Mental-model load — three editing spaces, set-level vs single-image edits.

The truth problem — internal QA language ("deformities," "artifacts") could never ship, but its substance had to.

The trust problem — we couldn't recommend one option over another. And in a stakeholder dry-run, my own pros/cons slides were called out as quietly steering the room.

My role

Content designer owning the product's language across UI and design reviews: taxonomy, CTAs, system-response vocabulary, guidance and error copy, download expectation-setting, and the review decks themselves — plus the content strategy that taught users what they could do and how, across prompt input and every editing control. Much of the UI followed from it. Direction presented up to the executive level through launch.

The taxonomy: three verbs that carry scope

The core deliverable was one sentence. Refine is additive and set-level; Modify is precise and single-image — the word pair signals "all images vs one image" without explanatory UI. From it came a locked CTA verb system and a placement rule: outside the editor, copy sets expectations; inside the editor, copy drives action.

Language systemsHonest AI disclosureDesign-review craftProbabilistic UX
Exhibit 2A — The three spaces
Generate
explores ideas
New set from prompt
Refine
shapes a set
All images · additive
Modify
perfects one image
Single image · precise

"Generate explores ideas. Refine shapes a set. Modify perfects one image."

Exhibit 2B — The locked side-rail vocabulary: noun + past participle, two words, no synonyms. Open a rejected label to see why it died.

Landed: Edit applied Variations created Image restored Image extended Background removed

Change saved
Imports a document-editor mental model into a probabilistic tool. Nothing here is "saved" the way a document is.
Original restored
Factually wrong — restore returns to the state a prompt produced, not the original; three restores may never reach the original. This copy review caught a functional misunderstanding the team itself held.
Regenerate
Model-centric; implies starting over rather than moving forward from what you have.

System responses repeat dozens of times per session. Humanized copy ("Your changes are live!") reads as fake when stacked in a feed — so the grammar is deliberately robotic-safe, on purpose.

Download copy: from selling to describing

The download modal iterated from overpromise ("Upscaled without changing your image" — false) toward a neutral system. The breakthrough was reframing: these are different behaviors, not pros and cons. "May change small features" isn't a con, and the ✓/✕ icons were quietly telling users what to pick.

I dropped the judgment framing for a "What to expect" expander — both options written at equal length so neither reads as the winner.

Never ship the lab notebook. Never hide its conclusions.

That "Details" line in the mock is exactly that rule at work — internal QA truth, translated into user language.

The sandbagging fix

In a design-review dry-run, a design leader called out that one option's cons looked self-created — sandbagging. Rather than soften the deck, I diagnosed the mechanics: asymmetric framing, judgment words, quantity imbalance, softeners on one side only.

Then I rewrote all five option slides symmetric and neutral, and built parallel framing sets so tone can swap without changing substance. The method was adopted into the team's design-review format.

Your modified image
Higher resolution — 4096 × 2732 px · JPEG

Upscaled to 4K with minimal changes.

What to expect
What happens — Increases image size to 4K; preserves the original look as closely as possible.
Details — Fine textures may appear slightly smoother or softer.
Best for — When you need a larger size with minimal visual change.
Enhanced detail — 2048 × 1536 px · JPEG

Enhances detail and texture—may change small features.

What to expect
What happens — Adds sharper, more detailed textures.
Details — Small features (like skin, hair, or text) may look slightly different.
Best for — When you want sharper detail, even if some features are reinterpreted.

Exhibit 2C — The final neutral download module, working. Both options same length, one benefit + one trade-off each, no icons implying a winner. (Yes, the expander expands.)

Exhibit 2D — The sandbagging rewrite (option names generalized)
Rejected — reads as a verdictLanded — describes the system
Assumes everyone wants modify on the results pagePrioritizes "modify" as a primary action within the results page
Easiest lift for developmentRequires minimal development changes
Scope creepIntroduces additional feature scope
Exhibit 2E — Where the language moved the design

The clearest case on this site of words replacing interface.

Refine vs Modify carry scope inside the verb
→
No explanatory UI needed for "all images vs one image" — two navigation labels do the teaching a helper panel would have done.Language as information architecture; adopted into the product's three-space structure
"These are different behaviors, not pros and cons"
→
The ✓/✕ icons left the download module, both options set at equal length, and a "What to expect" expander replaced the visual verdict.A reframing argument that redesigned a component
Outside the editor, copy sets expectations; inside, copy drives action
→
A placement rule deciding where guidance UI may live on screen — before design debates started, not after.With the product designers; the sandbagging rewrite did the same for the team's review decks

Outcomes

  • The locked vocabulary and helper map moved to engineering handoff; the download copy system was delivered verbatim for the alignment meeting gating dev-spec dates.
  • The honest-framing method was adopted into the team's design-review format.
  • The copy process itself surfaced one production bug and one spec/claim contradiction before they reached customers.

"Knowing when not to be human is senior judgment. Repeated system responses are infrastructure — delight stacked five deep reads as fake."What Chapter 02 taught me

03
Chapter 03 · Samsung Knox · this one runs dark, like the subject

The language of no: trust and guardrails for an enterprise AI assistant

UX content owner, AI assistant trust surface · Knox admin console · 2025 – 2026
The 60-second versionfull chapter ≈ 4 min
The problem
An AI assistant heading into a security console's beta with raw refusals — block messages that convicted the user on every false positive.
The move
Thirteen guardrail messages, each traceable to its source. A disclaimer restructure legal said yes to. One "may" doing the ethical work.
The result
Nine messages shipped in beta. A false feature promise became honest retention copy. 46 screens reviewed, ~55 findings.

The challenge

Samsung Knox is the enterprise security platform IT admins use to manage fleets of corporate devices. The team was putting an AI assistant into that admin console, where a wrong answer can misconfigure security policy for thousands of phones. Over-trust (acting without review) is as much a failure mode here as under-trust (never adopting).

Heading into the beta, the guardrail tickets had no user-facing messages — interventions would have surfaced as raw refusals. The live prototype's block message read "your request contains content that violates our policies," which convicts the user on every false positive.

The entry screen stacked five disclaimers into legal wallpaper — and promised a session-export feature that didn't exist.

My role

UX content owner for the assistant's trust surface: sole author of the guardrail messaging document (v1–v4), the naming strategy, and the measurement framework; negotiator of disclaimer and consent copy with legal, design, PM, and engineering; and owner of a 46-screen copy review that set house style rules. The assistant's content strategy was 0→1 work — there was no playbook here before this one.

Enterprise AISafety & refusal UXLegal negotiationMeasurement

Untangling what the system actually does

I started by asking why there were so many guardrail triggers — and traced every one to an engineering ticket, a stakeholder comment, or a section of the internal guardrail policy. The provenance table went into the messaging doc itself, so every message could be defended by pointing at its source.

The audit caught a real conflation: one "rate limit" message was doing three different jobs. I separated input limit, output limit, and runtime limit — each with recovery advice the user can actually follow.

The old copy told admins to "wait and try again" — right for throttling, wrong for the other two. Untangling system truth before writing copy is the job.

Refusal moments are where trust is won or lost — and exactly where teams spend the least design time. Nobody demos the block message.

Interactive — Fix this message. The live prototype's block message, with its problems marked. Tap each one, then rewrite it.
"I'm sorry, your request contains content that violates our policies."

Tap a highlighted problem ↑

"Your message could not be processed because it may violate our content policy. Please rephrase your request and try again."

The "may" does the ethical work — it covers false positives without withholding the reason, and the recovery path is one the user can actually take. Constraints this sentence survived: one configurable message for all filter types, a security rule against revealing which guardrail fired, a stakeholder demand for a reason, and a user who deserves not to be accused.

Exhibit 3A — One message, three jobs: the limits untangled
LimitBefore — one message for all threeAfter — advice the user can actually follow
Input limit"You've reached the request limit. Please wait a moment and try again."
Right for throttling; wrong for everything else.		Your message is too long to process. Try shortening it or breaking it into smaller questions.
Output limitMy response was cut short because it exceeded the output limit. Try asking a more specific question so I can keep the answer shorter.
Runtime limitThis request was stopped because it used more resources than allowed. Try breaking it into smaller steps.

Disclaimers, consent, and the legal negotiation

The entry screen stacked terms language, data-usage language, accuracy hedges, a translation warning, and an input warning. I lost the push to cut legally required lines — and won the fight that mattered: structure. One tightened paragraph up top, one persistent line under the input at the moment it matters.

Pitching the change as structure-not-content is why legal said yes.

I rejected the translation warning's "use English for best results" — it reads as English-superiority and breaks when the user already is asking in English. A verb-level analysis landed: "[The assistant] supports English. Translated responses may be less accurate."

I also caught a trust-breaking claim — that promised export feature — and replaced it with honest 30-day retention copy.

Naming and measurement

The renaming brief came with hard constraints (no "AI" in the name, must fit the product family). I rebuilt the strategy on naming research; three team favorites died on receipts, not taste: a trademark collision, a toxic association, a partner's product name.

For measurement, I wrote the framework that answers "what's the KPI?" with there isn't one — four pillars, with guardrail metrics first-class (rollback frequency, bulk actions without review). Success is not speed at the cost of control.

Exhibit 3B — The entry screen, restructured (diagram)

I lost the push to cut legally required lines — and won the structure. Same content, different architecture; that's why legal said yes.

Exhibit 3C — Where the language moved the design

In an enterprise security product, the content decisions were system decisions.

The structure-not-content pitch to legal
→
The entry screen was re-laid-out: five stacked disclaimers became one tightened paragraph up top plus one persistent line under the input, at the moment it matters.Negotiated with legal, built with design — the layout was the compromise
One "rate limit" message was doing three jobs
→
Three distinct error states surfaced in the UI, each with recovery advice a user can actually follow — a system distinction the single message had been hiding.With engineering, traced through their own tickets
Every guardrail message carries its provenance
→
Messages became reviewable design objects — mapped across five engineering tickets, each defensible by pointing at its source.With PM and engineering; the table lives in the spec, not a side doc

Outcomes

  • 13 guardrail messages finalized and mapped across five engineering tickets; 9 shipped for the beta, 2 consciously parked with tracking tickets.
  • Legal accepted the disclaimer restructure; the false export claim became honest 30-day retention copy.
  • A 46-screen copy review produced ~55 findings — including that all 46 screens had zero error, empty, or permission states.

"With legal, pitch structure, not content. That one sentence has paid for itself a dozen times."What Chapter 03 taught me

Further studies · 04 – 07

The supporting evidence

Four more, told shorter — each one ending with what it cost me to learn. Full versions available in conversation, where the good details live anyway.

Study 04 · Samsung Knox

Six thousand terms, seven products, one vocabulary

Seven enterprise consoles had drifted into seven dialects — "EMM" meant "UEM" meant "MDM," sometimes in the same product. The audit strategy was mine end to end; I built the suite's first complete terminology inventory, then handed the framework to fellow content designers, who ran it on their own consoles.

Audit excerpt, recreated — the match-type taxonomy that classifies variants instead of erasing them.

  • Capture, don't fix — every term logged exactly as shipped, because mixing inventory with improvement produces neither.
  • An AI-assisted deduplication pipeline that failed twice (inflating to 7,242 rows, then over-collapsing to 222 clusters) before the version that classified variants instead of erasing them — with a human between the algorithm and every merge.
  • ~184 glossary entries into structured review; decision frameworks for the contested terms ("agent" over "client"; plan vs bundle vs edition; "a policy applies one or more settings to a device").
  • Proved the model on a bounded surface: the 86-tooltip audit, every item with a disposition and a ticket.

"AI clustering fails silently in both directions. The fix is the same either way: keep the variants visible, and put a human between the algorithm and the merge."

Study 05 · Samsung Knox

One rule, thirteen responses: a design system for an agentic AI

An assistant that acts — pulling logs, re-queuing firmware, drafting policy — can't let "here's my plan" be mistaken for "I already did it." I designed the response system: 13 classes, a classification router, seven reusable chassis, and a model-facing runtime contract. This was 0→1 content strategy for the assistant — built, then socialized: cross-functional partners first, then directors, VPs, and executives for buy-in on direction.

The router, diagrammed — four questions classify any response into its class.

  • The unblock: design was struggling to support the assistant at all — until this framework proposed a reusable component system, paired with prompt-layer content telling the model how to use it. That pairing is what moved the project forward.
  • The one rule: primary-action styling is reserved for the single action that changes system state. Everything else is a reply or a utility. Approval on a plan is a reply; the button appears only at the true commit.
  • A four-question router classifies any response: changes state? needs input? safe without approval? produces a deliverable?
  • The taxonomy rewritten as an engineering contract — required/forbidden fields per class, derivation over introspection — adoptable because it ceded the enforcement mechanism.
  • Ten operational safety concerns collapsed into a Denied class (neutral, never red, never apologizes, always carries a route), three Confirm tiers, and honest partial-completion states.

"Content design at its most leveraged isn't writing strings — it's defining the taxonomy that determines which strings can exist."

Study 06 · Autodesk

The chatbot wasn't the problem. The missing judgment system was.

Everyone could tell the internal AI support assistant was sometimes wrong; nobody could say how, how often, or in which ways. I wrote the evaluation framework — then ran the pilot audit and caught the auditor AI hallucinating its own findings. Same pattern as Knox: I'm shaping the assistant's experience direction, not reporting on it — stakeholder buy-in run up to the exec level, and engineering relationships built where none existed.

One scored thread from the pilot — the diagnosis the taxonomy made possible.

  • A 15-point LLM-as-judge rubric across five dimensions, with fabricated information an automatic zero and a critical-failure flag for hallucinations and broken escalations.
  • A five-category failure taxonomy — because a hallucinated deadline, a tone complaint, and a dead-end fallback all need different fixes.
  • The pilot's best diagnosis: a 9/15 thread whose root cause wasn't hallucination but state management — the bot regenerated everything each turn instead of applying the user's edits.
  • The meta-lesson institutionalized: judge output is untrusted until human-calibrated (κ ≥ 0.7), and every judgment carries row-level provenance.

"Evaluation is content design. Deciding what a good fallback looks like is language work — expressed as a rubric instead of microcopy."

Study 07 · iStock

Renaming a product line without breaking trust

Two collections renamed, a benefit removed, grandfathering rules to explain — a trust minefield where one wrong phrase had real money consequences. I owned the customer-facing copy: 158 text nodes, one source of truth, no euphemisms.

The conditional-messaging matrix — writing the logic as well as the strings.

  • Killed "or you switch plans" everywhere — it overstated the loss, and overstating a loss is as much a trust failure as hiding one. It would also have suppressed the exact upgrade behavior the project existed to encourage.
  • One sanctioned wording for the rollover rule, word-for-word identical across every FAQ, tile, tooltip, and modal that states it.
  • Caught an inverted rename mapping in stakeholder-approved copy — a one-word typo that would have reversed the entire rename.
  • Specified the conditional-messaging logic (plan tier × grandfathering status), because content design here meant writing the logic as well as the strings.

"Precision and kindness turned out to be the same edit — the softer drafts weren't gentler, they were wrong."

The through-line · 2014 – 2026

The material never changed. What it operates on did.

Twelve years of the same craft — language — applied one layer deeper into the machine each time. This is why the AI work isn't a pivot. It's the same job, five levels down — and the descent is deliberate. This is the long game.

2014
Words organize a catalog.
Best Buy — taxonomy, IA, and content strategy for a product catalog at national scale. Language as structure.
2018
Words become a conversation.
Lemonade — Maya, an AI insurance chatbot. The first time my words were performed by a machine instead of read beside one.
2024
Words explain the model.
Getty Images — generative tools where the copy's job is honest expectation-setting for probabilistic behavior: what changed, why, and what it costs.
2025
Words constrain the model.
Samsung Knox — guardrail messaging, refusal language, consent, and a response grammar the model itself is contractually held to.
2026
Words judge the model.
Autodesk — evaluation rubrics, failure taxonomies, and audit workflows: deciding what "good" means, then measuring the machine against it.

Structure → conversation → explanation → constraint → judgment. I didn't move toward AI. I kept doing content design while its subject matter became AI.

Field notes

Things I believe, learned the hard way

Every one of these came out of a real product decision — most of them out of getting it wrong first.

01

Clarity can't override truth.

The clearest label in testing was legally wrong. The craft is the phrasing that's both — and it always takes more rounds than either alone.

02

Disabled, never unavailable.

"Unavailable" implies broken. "Disabled" is intentional and temporary — and it always gets a reason. Users forgive limits; they don't forgive shrugs.

03

Say it once, in the right place.

If the overlay says it and the note says it, we don't need the tooltip saying it a third way. Redundancy isn't reinforcement; it's noise.

04

Never ship the lab notebook. Never hide its conclusions.

"Deformities" and "artifacts" can't reach users — but their substance must. Translating internal truth into user language is the whole job.

05

Robotic on purpose.

System responses that repeat fifty times a session are infrastructure, not conversation. Two words, noun + past participle, no synonyms — and no "Your changes are live!"

06

Honest process is a system property.

My "sandbagged" slides weren't dishonest in intent — they were asymmetric in mechanics. Fix the framework and the honesty follows.

The complete record · 2014 – 2026

The ledger

Everything, not just the highlights — because a portfolio that only shows three projects is a trailer, and this is the filmography. Ask me about any line.

2010
From the archive · 2019 – 2020

Paper trail.

Deliverables from the agency years, kept because they still argue well: experience maps, personas, content strategies. Treated as artifacts — because that’s what deliverables become. Evidence that the thinking happened. Drag or swipe sideways.

The enterprise-era artifacts — glossary workbooks, response-system specs, audit pipelines — live under NDA.
Ask, and I’ll walk you through sanitized versions.
About

The breadth is the point.

I'm a self-taught content designer from Vancouver who worked from Best Buy's product catalog to running content design for AI products at three major companies at once. No degree — just twelve years of treating every complex system as something that can be made legible, and building the system that makes it so.

And the AI thread isn't new: I was writing Maya, Lemonade's insurance chatbot, back in 2018 — before "conversational AI" had a design discipline attached to it. Lemonade taught me a company can talk like a person. Enterprise taught me exactly when it shouldn't.

My instinct when facing chaos is to build a framework that makes the chaos navigable: glossaries, taxonomies, response systems, audit pipelines, measurement frameworks. I win through thoroughness, not flash — the signature work here isn't a beautiful screen, it's six thousand terms catalogued so that seven products finally speak one language.

And I treat feedback about my work the way I treat feedback about copy: as data. The sandbagging story in Chapter 02 is on this site because it started with someone telling me my slides were biased.

Working style — state a point of view, then open the door: "here's my recommendation and why — choose what fits."
Operating pattern — 0→1 strategy, socialized upward: cross-functional partners first, then directors, VPs, and executives for buy-in. I build the system the next hundred decisions come from, then hand it over — it keeps working after my engagement ends.
Current arena — AI products, because probabilistic systems need honest language more than any interface ever has.
Also — I teach IA and content design at Vancouver Film School, run a small Etsy shop, and write songs. Ask about the aquarium.

Let's make something complicated make sense.

Zameer Ali · Senior Content Designer · Vancouver, BC · open to remote (Canada) Zameer_Ali@live.com · linkedin.com/in/zameer-ali · the 90-second version

Public-facing work: gettyimages.ca/ai/generation · istockphoto.com/ai · shopify.com/ca/magic · shopify.com/ca/capital · samsungknox.com (say hi to the Guide)

Colophon — the design follows the content rules it describes. Two typefaces, two registers: Fraunces speaks, Space Grotesk labels — narrative voice and system voice, kept apart the way they should be in product. One meaning per color, the rule from Chapter 03's product applied to its own portfolio: teal is the only accent; red touches nothing but rejected copy, green nothing but what shipped. The "show the fix" exhibits make you sit with the broken version first, because that's the order users experience it in. And there's no decoration anywhere — every image is a real artifact, every big visual is a word or a number. Content design is the art direction.

Built AI-first — researched, drafted, and assembled with AI agents working from my own project notes, then edited by hand. It's the same way I work in product: the tools do the assembly; the judgment stays mine. Employer-confidential details are generalized throughout; I go deeper in conversation.
